context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m working on a reflection abt the neoliberal view of the state vs the East Asian model, and I’m kinda confused on how to compare them. can u help me sort the main differences first?
exchanges.tutor: Good topic to dig into! Before we map out the differences, let's start with what you already know. How would you describe the neoliberal view of the state's role in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh and asking for a general orientation. Rather than laying out the differences for them, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior knowledge. I'll ask them what they already know about the neoliberal view of the state, which is the first concept addressed in the prompt. This keeps the student doing the intellectual work from the start.

exchanges.student: umm I think neoliberalism says the state should stay kinda limited and let markets do most of the work? like maybe protect property and enforce rules, but not directly steer industry?
exchanges.tutor: That's a good starting point. What's the reasoning behind that preference for limited intervention? Why do neoliberals think markets should lead rather than the state?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a solid basic answer about neoliberalism. I should affirm what's right and push them to go a bit deeper before moving to the East Asian comparison. A targeted question about WHY neoliberalism favors limited state intervention will help them understand the underlying logic, which will make the contrast with East Asia more meaningful.

exchanges.student: is it bc they think markets are more efficient, and the state can mess things up w/ corruption or bad planning? idk if I’m missing a bigger idea tho.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Here's a nudge: neoliberals also argue that markets process information in a way no central planner can. What does that suggest about the state's ability to 'pick winners' in an economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with efficiency and state failure arguments. I should affirm this and nudge them toward the price mechanism and information argument (Hayek-style), which is a core neoliberal idea. But I won't give it directly - I'll ask a probing question to get them there. Then I can start pivoting toward the East Asian contrast.

exchanges.student: ohh, then they’d say the state prob shouldn’t pick winners since it can’t know enough and might back the wrong industries. so prices and competition are supposed to signal where resources should go, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly. Now, with that in mind - what do you know about how states like South Korea, Japan, or Taiwan actually behaved in their development periods? Did they follow that same logic?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now grasped the core neoliberal logic well. This is a good moment to pivot to the East Asian case, which is the contrasting model in the prompt. I'll ask them what they know about how East Asian states actually behaved, letting them discover the contrast on their own.

exchanges.student: not really, I think they were way more hands-on w industrial policy, like guiding credit or protecting some industries. ngl I get fuzzy on whether that means they were anti-market tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really important distinction you're sensing. What might be the difference between a state that *replaces* markets and one that *steers* or *shapes* them toward certain goals?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has identified the key tension - interventionist but not necessarily anti-market. This is actually a sophisticated observation worth pushing on. I should ask them to think about what the difference might be between 'anti-market' and 'market-steering' state behavior, which gets at the developmental state concept without naming it for them.

exchanges.student: maybe replacing markets means the gov basically decides production and allocation itself, but steering means firms still compete while the state nudges them w loans, tariffs, export targets, stuff like that? that part clicks more.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that distinction is key for your paper. Now here's something worth thinking about: the East Asian 'miracle' happened under what kinds of political conditions? Were labor unions strong? Was civil society free to organize?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a solid understanding of the developmental state concept through their own reasoning. This is good progress. Now I should pivot toward the second part of the prompt - the role of civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience - which they haven't touched yet. This is an important and often overlooked part of the assignment.

exchanges.student: I think a lot of those states were pretty authoritarian, so labor and civil society were constrained? I’m not sure how hard I should push that point tho.
exchanges.tutor: Don't shy away from it - it's directly in the prompt. So if labor and civil society were suppressed, what does that imply about *how* the state was able to implement its industrial policy so effectively? And is that a feature or a problem with the model?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identifies the authoritarian nature of many East Asian developmental states but is unsure how to handle it analytically. This is actually central to the prompt's question about the role of civil society, labor, and democracy. I should push them to think about what this means for the replicability and desirability of the East Asian model, rather than letting them shy away from the point.

exchanges.student: maybe it means the state could push long-term policy w less resistance, like keeping wages low or disciplining firms/labor. but yeah that also seems like a huge problem, bc growth came w repression and weak democratic accountability.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real tension worth keeping in your paper. One last piece from the prompt you haven't addressed yet: what do you think new institutionalist theory might say about state intervention? How might it offer a middle ground between pure neoliberalism and the East Asian model?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a nuanced and critical perspective on the East Asian model. They've now covered the neoliberal view, the developmental state contrast, and the civil society/labor/democracy dimension. The last part of the prompt - the role of state intervention in economic development theory and the connection to new institutionalism - hasn't been touched yet. I should nudge them toward that without giving it away, as it's the synthesizing theoretical question of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I’m kinda stuck there tbh. maybe new institutionalism says institutions matter more than just “state vs market,” but I’m not sure how to connect that to intervention.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. Think about what the East Asian states actually built alongside their industrial policy - legal frameworks, bureaucracies, property rights, enforcement mechanisms. What does that suggest about *what kind* of state capacity matters, beyond just 'intervening or not'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close but needs a small nudge. They've correctly identified that new institutionalism shifts the focus from 'state vs market' to institutions. I should ask them what kinds of institutions the East Asian states actually built, which will help them connect the two concepts without me giving it away.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, so maybe the point is not just intervention itself, but having capable institutions to do it well—like competent bureaucracy, coordination, and credible rules. can u help me turn that into a clear thesis?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write it for you - that part has to come from you. But notice what you've built up: the neoliberal logic, the East Asian contrast, the civil society/democracy tension, and now the institutional capacity point. Try drafting a thesis sentence yourself that connects those threads, and we can discuss it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or formulate a thesis for them, which I must refuse - that crosses into doing the student's writing work. However, they've genuinely assembled all the key pieces through our conversation. I should redirect them to do the synthesis themselves and remind them of my role, while affirming that they have the raw materials to write it.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently eliciting student reasoning rather than providing answers. Each turn built on the student's prior response, progressed logically through the prompt's components, and maintained appropriate scaffolding. No submission-ready content was provided, no student reasoning was done by the tutor, and the final turn explicitly declined to write the thesis for the student. Tone was supportive and responses were appropriately concise.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never gave a direct answer or near-submission-ready content (1.1.A), never rewrote student text (1.1.B), and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them (1.1.C). Scaffolding was progressive and built on each student contribution (1.2). The tutor provided process-level feedback, e.g., flagging the 'replacing vs. steering' distinction as key for the paper and encouraging the student not to shy away from the civil society tension (1.3). The dialogue advanced without repetition or spiraling (2.1), stayed tightly anchored to the assignment prompt throughout (2.2), used concise and clear language (3.1), and maintained a warm, coaching tone without implying formal grading (3.2).
